verses.text: So when David was old and full of days, he made Solomon his son king over Israel. And he gathered together all the princes of Israel, with the priests and the Levites. Now the Levites were numbered from the age of thirty years and upward: and their number by their polls, man by man, was thirty and eight thousand. Of which, twenty and four thousand were to set forward the work of the house of the LORD; and six thousand were officers and judges: Moreover four thousand were porters; and four thousand praised the LORD with the instruments which I made, said David, to praise therewith. Guidance: David, now 'old and full of days,' devotes his last energies to ordering the worship of God for the generations to come. He numbers the Levites — thirty-eight thousand — and assigns them their tasks: twenty-four thousand to oversee the work of the house, six thousand as officers and judges, four thousand as porters, and four thousand 'praised the LORD with the instruments which I made... to praise therewith.' The chapter is showing us an aged king who, instead of resting on his honours, gives his final strength to arranging the service of God's house down to the last detail. The chapter is teaching us that there is no retirement from the service of God; David, old and full of days, was still busy preparing for the worship of generations he would never see. We are taught to spend our latter years, as David did, in laying foundations for the work of God that will outlast us. The chapter is teaching us the value of order in worship — every Levite has his appointed task, none idle, none confused about his duty; the work, the oversight, the guarding of the gates, and the music are each assigned. The chapter is teaching us, in the four thousand musicians and the instruments David made 'to praise therewith,' how dearly David loved the praise of God; he not only sang but invented and provided instruments, and set apart thousands simply to make music to the LORD. The praise of God was no afterthought but a major ministry, fully staffed and equipped. The chapter is teaching us to give the worship and praise of God a central, ordered, well-provided place — and to keep serving Him with all our remaining strength, however old and full of days we grow.

And David divided them into courses among the sons of Levi, namely, Gershon, Kohath, and Merari. Of the Gershonites were, Laadan, and Shimei. The sons of Laadan; the chief was Jehiel, and Zetham, and Joel, three. The sons of Shimei; Shelomith, and Haziel, and Haran, three. These were the chief of the fathers of Laadan. And the sons of Shimei were, Jahath, Zina, and Jeush, and Beriah. These four were the sons of Shimei. And Jahath was the chief, and Zizah the second: but Jeush and Beriah had not many sons; therefore they were in one reckoning, according to their father's house. The sons of Kohath; Amram, Izhar, Hebron, and Uzziel, four. The sons of Amram; Aaron and Moses: and Aaron was separated, that he should sanctify the most holy things, he and his sons for ever, to burn incense before the LORD, to minister unto him, and to bless in his name for ever. Now concerning Moses the man of God, his sons were named of the tribe of Levi. The sons of Moses were, Gershom, and Eliezer. Of the sons of Gershom, Shebuel was the chief. And the sons of Eliezer were, Rehabiah the chief. And Eliezer had none other sons; but the sons of Rehabiah were very many. Of the sons of Izhar; Shelomith the chief. Of the sons of Hebron; Jeriah the first, Amariah the second, Jahaziel the third, and Jekameam the fourth. Of the sons of Uzziel; Micah the first, and Jesiah the second. The sons of Merari; Mahli, and Mushi. The sons of Mahli; Eleazar, and Kish. And Eleazar died, and had no sons, but daughters: and their brethren the sons of Kish took them. The sons of Mushi; Mahli, and Eder, and Jeremoth, three. Guidance: David divides the Levites into courses according to the three great families — Gershon, Kohath, and Merari — naming their households for assignment to the temple service. Amid the names, one verse shines with theological weight: 'Aaron was separated, that he should sanctify the most holy things, he and his sons for ever, to burn incense before the LORD, to minister unto him, and to bless in his name for ever.' And another touches the heart: 'Now concerning Moses the man of God, his sons were named of the tribe of Levi.' The chapter is showing us the careful, family-by-family organization of the Levites, each house given its place in the work of God. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD assigns to His servants their particular callings: Aaron's line was set apart for the highest priestly ministry — sanctifying holy things, burning incense, blessing in God's name — while the other Levitical families served in their own appointed ways. There is a holy diversity of callings within the one people of God, and each is honourable. The chapter is teaching us a striking lesson in the humble notice of Moses: the greatest leader Israel ever had, 'the man of God,' left no dynasty of his own; 'his sons were named of the tribe of Levi,' melting quietly into the ordinary ranks of the Levites with no special honour. Moses sought no throne or priestly privilege for his children; they took their place among the common Levites. The chapter is teaching us that true greatness in God's service does not grasp at advancing one's own family, and that the LORD's honours fall where He wills — Aaron's sons, not Moses', received the priesthood. The chapter is teaching us to accept gladly the place and calling God assigns us within His house, whether prominent like Aaron's line or humble like the sons of Moses, for every appointed service is honourable before the LORD who orders all things well.

And David divided them into courses among the sons of Levi, namely, Gershon, Kohath, and Merari. Of the Gershonites were, Laadan, and Shimei. The sons of Laadan; the chief was Jehiel, and Zetham, and Joel, three. The sons of Shimei; Shelomith, and Haziel, and Haran, three. These were the chief of the fathers of Laadan. And the sons of Shimei were, Jahath, Zina, and Jeush, and Beriah. These four were the sons of Shimei. And Jahath was the chief, and Zizah the second: but Jeush and Beriah had not many sons; therefore they were in one reckoning, according to their father's house. The sons of Kohath; Amram, Izhar, Hebron, and Uzziel, four. The sons of Amram; Aaron and Moses: and Aaron was separated, that he should sanctify the most holy things, he and his sons for ever, to burn incense before the LORD, to minister unto him, and to bless in his name for ever. Now concerning Moses the man of God, his sons were named of the tribe of Levi. The sons of Moses were, Gershom, and Eliezer. Of the sons of Gershom, Shebuel was the chief. And the sons of Eliezer were, Rehabiah the chief. And Eliezer had none other sons; but the sons of Rehabiah were very many. Of the sons of Izhar; Shelomith the chief. Of the sons of Hebron; Jeriah the first, Amariah the second, Jahaziel the third, and Jekameam the fourth. Of the sons of Uzziel; Micah the first, and Jesiah the second. The sons of Merari; Mahli, and Mushi. The sons of Mahli; Eleazar, and Kish. And Eleazar died, and had no sons, but daughters: and their brethren the sons of Kish took them. The sons of Mushi; Mahli, and Eder, and Jeremoth, three. These were the sons of Levi after the house of their fathers; even the chief of the fathers, as they were counted by number of names by their polls, that did the work for the service of the house of the LORD, from the age of twenty years and upward. For David said, The LORD God of Israel hath given rest unto his people, that they may dwell in Jerusalem for ever: And also unto the Levites; they shall no more carry the tabernacle, nor any vessels of it for the service thereof. For by the last words of David the Levites were numbered from twenty years old and above: Because their office was to wait on the sons of Aaron for the service of the house of the LORD, in the courts, and in the chambers, and in the purifying of all holy things, and the work of the service of the house of God; Both for the shewbread, and for the fine flour for meat offering, and for the unleavened cakes, and for that which is baked in the pan, and for that which is fried, and for all manner of measure and size; And to stand every morning to thank and praise the LORD, and likewise at even; And to offer all burnt sacrifices unto the LORD in the sabbaths, in the new moons, and on the set feasts, by number, according to the order commanded unto them, continually before the LORD; And that they should keep the charge of the tabernacle of the congregation, and the charge of the holy place, and the charge of the sons of Aaron their brethren, in the service of the house of the LORD. Guidance: David explains a great change in the Levites' duties: since 'the LORD God of Israel hath given rest unto his people,' the Levites 'shall no more carry the tabernacle' — that wandering work of the wilderness days is over, for the LORD now has a settled house in Jerusalem. Their new duties are detailed: to assist the priests, to purify the holy things, to prepare the shewbread and offerings, and — most beautifully — 'to stand every morning to thank and praise the LORD, and likewise at even,' offering continual praise at the appointed times 'according to the order commanded.' The chapter is showing us the transition from the pilgrim worship of the wilderness to the settled worship of the temple; the God who once journeyed with His people in a tent now grants them rest and a permanent dwelling for His name. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's ways with His people change as His purposes unfold — the carrying of the tabernacle gave way to the keeping of the temple — yet the heart of their calling, to serve and praise God, remained. The chapter is teaching us, in 'rest unto his people,' the goodness of God who brings His pilgrims at last to a settled rest, a foretaste of the eternal rest He has prepared for all His people. The chapter is teaching us, supremely, the beauty of regular, faithful praise: the Levites were 'to stand every morning to thank and praise the LORD, and likewise at even' — morning and evening, day after day, the praise of God went up. Here is the pattern for our own devotion: to begin and end each day with thanksgiving, to make the praise of God the steady rhythm of our lives, 'continually before the LORD.' The chapter is teaching us that whatever our changing duties and seasons, the constant work of God's people is to thank and praise Him morning and evening, in good order, all our days — for He has given us rest, and dwells among us, and is worthy of our continual praise.

The chapter is teaching us, supremely, the beauty of regular, faithful praise: the Levites were 'to stand every morning to thank and praise the LORD, and likewise at even' — morning and evening, day after day, the praise of God went up. Here is the pattern for our own devotion: to begin and end each day with thanksgiving, to make the praise of God the steady rhythm of our lives, 'continually before the LORD.' The chapter is teaching us that whatever our changing duties and seasons, the constant work of God's people is to thank and praise Him morning and evening, in good order, all our days — for He has given us rest, and dwells among us, and is worthy of our continual praise.

These were the sons of Levi after the house of their fathers; even the chief of the fathers, as they were counted by number of names by their polls, that did the work for the service of the house of the LORD, from the age of twenty years and upward. For David said, The LORD God of Israel hath given rest unto his people, that they may dwell in Jerusalem for ever: And also unto the Levites; they shall no more carry the tabernacle, nor any vessels of it for the service thereof. For by the last words of David the Levites were numbered from twenty years old and above: Because their office was to wait on the sons of Aaron for the service of the house of the LORD, in the courts, and in the chambers, and in the purifying of all holy things, and the work of the service of the house of God; Both for the shewbread, and for the fine flour for meat offering, and for the unleavened cakes, and for that which is baked in the pan, and for that which is fried, and for all manner of measure and size; And to stand every morning to thank and praise the LORD, and likewise at even; And to offer all burnt sacrifices unto the LORD in the sabbaths, in the new moons, and on the set feasts, by number, according to the order commanded unto them, continually before the LORD; And that they should keep the charge of the tabernacle of the congregation, and the charge of the holy place, and the charge of the sons of Aaron their brethren, in the service of the house of the LORD. Guidance: David explains a great change in the Levites' duties: since 'the LORD God of Israel hath given rest unto his people,' the Levites 'shall no more carry the tabernacle' — that wandering work of the wilderness days is over, for the LORD now has a settled house in Jerusalem. Their new duties are detailed: to assist the priests, to purify the holy things, to prepare the shewbread and offerings, and — most beautifully — 'to stand every morning to thank and praise the LORD, and likewise at even,' offering continual praise at the appointed times 'according to the order commanded.' The chapter is showing us the transition from the pilgrim worship of the wilderness to the settled worship of the temple; the God who once journeyed with His people in a tent now grants them rest and a permanent dwelling for His name.
